answer:
In "The Seafarer" (Old English Elegies, ch. 5), the speaker, enduring voluntary exile on the sea, projects onto his lost homeland (the "strongholds" of middle-earth) images of communal warmth, earthly pleasures, and social bonds: the harp's music, rings bestowed by a lord, a wife's joy, mead-drinking, and laughter among kinsmen.[^1] These are contrasted sharply with the sea's desolation—no sheltering kin, only bird cries mistaken for human merriment—but the homeland remains a site of untested ease for the "blessed man" who dwells there.[^2] Ultimately, the speaker reorients longing toward a spiritual "home" in heaven, deeming earthly strongholds transient amid decaying kingdoms and inevitable death.[^3]

Iqbal's "Saqaliya" (Bang-E-Dara Part 2, ch. 21), by contrast, stages a collective exile from a civilizational homeland: Sicily as the "cradle" and "honor" of Hejazi (Arab-Islamic) culture, once a watery "desert" mastered by desert-dwellers whose ships treated seas as playgrounds, swords nested lightning, and presence trembled emperors' courts.[^4] The lost place evokes imperial renewal—a "dead world" revived, humanity freed from superstition—its "world-consuming beauty" like fire, now silenced in ruins whose very shores lament.[^5] Unlike the personal domesticity of the Seafarer's homeland, Iqbal's speaker projects epic, transformative power onto this Muslim Mediterranean past, mourning its grave while vowing to transplant its memory to Hindustan.

Both speakers idealize the absent homeland as a locus of lost vitality—the Seafarer's intimate and fading earthly ties, Iqbal's monumental cultural blaze—but pivot from despair: the former to Christian eternity, the latter to diasporic revival.[^6]

[^1]: Old English Elegies, [ch. 5](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/05-maey-ich-be-me-sylfum-soth-yied-wrekan-sithas-seya/translation/#p-549618).
[^2]: Old English Elegies, [ch. 5](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/05-maey-ich-be-me-sylfum-soth-yied-wrekan-sithas-seya/translation/#p-8c53e7).
[^3]: Old English Elegies, [ch. 5](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/05-maey-ich-be-me-sylfum-soth-yied-wrekan-sithas-seya/translation/#p-1c332d).
[^4]: Bang-E-Dara Part 2, [ch. 21](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-13e58a).
[^5]: Bang-E-Dara Part 2, [ch. 21](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-ab88c0).
[^6]: Old English Elegies, [ch. 5](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/05-maey-ich-be-me-sylfum-soth-yied-wrekan-sithas-seya/translation/#p-eff626); Bang-E-Dara Part 2, [ch. 21](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-82cbe3).
